running performance prediction algorithm VO2max endurance study
coverage_notes: Strong coverage across the full spectrum of race prediction approaches. Includes the foundational Riegel formula (1981), VDOT/Daniels validation (Scudamore 2018), empirical recreational runner models showing Riegel limitations (Vickers 2016), physiological power-based models (Mulligan 2018), big-data approaches with ~1.6M sessions (Emig 2020), machine learning recommender systems for marathon training (Smyth 2021), AI-based marathon prediction using KNN/ANN (Lerebourg 2023), and a meta-analysis of training determinants explaining 38-81% of marathon performance variance (Doherty 2020). Gap: Cameron's formula lacks peer-reviewed validation studies; it is primarily described on web resources without formal journal publication. The Dave Cameron model is referenced in comparison tools but has no citable DOI-bearing paper.

papers.title: Athletic Records and Human Endurance
papers.notes: The foundational paper introducing the Riegel formula T2 = T1 * (D2/D1)^1.06 for predicting race times across distances in the endurance range (3.5-230 minutes). Based on analysis of world records across running, swimming, and walking. Remains the most widely used race prediction formula in running calculators worldwide, though later work shows it is too optimistic for recreational runners at marathon distance.

papers.title: An empirical study of race times in recreational endurance runners
papers.journal: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
papers.notes: Analyzed 2,303 recreational runners and found that the Riegel formula underestimated marathon times by at least 10 minutes for half the participants. Developed improved prediction models incorporating sex, age, BMI, and training variables (tempo runs, weekly mileage, intervals) that achieved mean squared errors of 208-228 vs Riegel's 381. Critical evidence that classical formulas calibrated on elite runners perform poorly for recreational athletes.

papers.title: An Evaluation of Time-Trial-Based Predictions of VO2max and Recommended Training Paces for Collegiate and Recreational Runners
papers.journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
papers.notes: Only direct validation study of the Daniels VDOT system against laboratory-measured VO2max. Tested NCAA Division 1 athletes (n=11) and recreational runners (n=9). VDOT underestimated VO2max in both groups, with larger discrepancies in recreational runners. Threshold pace recommendations were reliable, but interval pace may not be optimal for improving VO2max in recreational runners. Key caution for coaching AI systems relying on VDOT-derived training paces.

papers.title: A minimal power model for human running performance
papers.notes: Derives the observed logarithmic scaling between world record running speeds and times from first principles of metabolic power supply. The model describes female and male record performances from 800m to marathon with mean errors of less than 1%. Introduces a quantitative definition of endurance using a relative metabolic power scale, providing a physiological basis for race prediction superior to purely empirical formulas like Riegel's.

papers.title: An evaluation of the training determinants of marathon performance: A meta-analysis with meta-regression
papers.journal: Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 85 studies with 137 runner cohorts examining which training variables predict marathon performance. Weekly running distance, number of runs per week, longest run, runs over 32km, and average training pace all showed significant negative associations with marathon finish time (R-squared 0.38-0.81). The non-finisher rate was 7.27%. Essential evidence for building training-based marathon prediction models rather than relying solely on race-to-race extrapolation.

papers.title: Human running performance from real-world big data
papers.notes: Applied a physiological power model to ~1.6 million running sessions from ~14,000 individuals, achieving race time predictions with mean error of only 2%. Uses two parameters — aerobic power index and endurance index — to characterize individual runners. The endurance parameter enables identification of lactate threshold from training data alone. Demonstrates that combining physiological models with big training data produces far more accurate individual predictions than population-based formulas.

papers.title: Recommendations for marathon runners: on the application of recommender systems and machine learning to support recreational marathon runners
papers.notes: Large-scale study using 1.86 million Strava training and race activities from 31,221 marathon runners (Dublin, London, New York 2014-2017). Best gradient-boosted model predicted marathon times within 14.34 minutes (<6% error). Demonstrates case-based reasoning for personalized training recommendations by matching runners to similar historical profiles. Directly relevant to building a Strava-based coaching AI that generates race predictions from training logs.

papers.title: Prediction of Marathon Performance using Artificial Intelligence
papers.notes: Compared k-nearest neighbor (KNN) and artificial neural network (ANN) for marathon prediction using 10km race time, BMI, age, and sex as inputs from 820 French runners. KNN achieved mean absolute error of 2.4% vs ANN's 5.6%, with both showing very high correlation with actual times (r>0.90). Demonstrates that simple ML models with minimal inputs (a recent 10K time plus basic demographics) can predict marathon performance with clinically useful accuracy for coaching applications.
